description: Apollo 17 Flight Journal - Day 5, part 4: Settling into lunar orbit

tokens: Apollo 17 is on its third orbit around the Moon and has just reacquired communication with Earth as it came around the eastern limb.
While around the far side, the CSM's main engine was fired for 22 seconds to lower the spacecraft's nearside apolune down to become a 14.3-nautical-mile (26.5-kilometre) perilune.
Immediately, Jack, ever the scientist, begins geological observations.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: There seem to be two general kinds of ray patterns: those associated with a lot of secondaries and light colored, and those that have no visible secondaries.
And that's independent, yet, from the irregular light-colored areas we've been calling swirls.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Houston, there also - a lot more - there is - there is a lot more of that light-colored swirl-like irregular material, or discoloration - if - whatever you want to call it, in the back side highlands, particularly as we approach Marginis, than I had previously gathered from the available photography.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And there still seems to be no relief associated with it.
Although, in many cases, it seems to follow ridge lines, or crater rims part way.
In other cases, it's quite irregular in its distribution.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And bank A, that time, with the chamber pressure, was up to 95.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And we got tracking data on you; has a 13.1 perigee - peri - perilune, rather.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Later on this pass, the PAO officer will state the current orbital dimensions as 58.9 by 14.3 nautical miles (109.1 by 26.5 km).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, that's great, Bob, and we're still looking at that zero bias on the DC meter of about 5 to 7 psi.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Some of the boys might be interested to know that in a place where the Sun is just grazing the slope - it's a steep slope on the north rim of Crisium, I can see the horizontal lineaments that were such a controversy on 15.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: [Garble] big one with the central peak.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: There are some very steep slopes that just have grazing Sun on them now, and with the binocs, you can see that horizontal lineation pattern.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: During Apollo 15, CMP Al Worden mentioned seeing layering in the sides of two major craters within Mare Crisium, Picard and Peirce, the latter of which does have a small central peak.
It may be Peirce that Jack is referring to.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, I've got the edge of Crisium now.
[Garble.] Getting hills on the side of Crisium, there.
[Pause.] Sure a lot smoother-looking material than I thought it would be.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Ron, you're about 1 minute from T-horizon.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: And, Ron, we - we're copying you on VOX, we believe.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: You're not supposed to copy the other guys, though.
Are you copying the other guys?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: I may have been talking loud.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, that's pointing at the horizon all right.
I don't see the crater, yet.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, I can see the rims of Microbus [sic] A and B.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: The audio recording has a series of repeated sounds, each about three or four seconds long with ticks at one-second intervals.
This is likely to be the sound of a Maurer 16mm movie camera being operated for a few seconds at a time with its frame-rate set to one frame per second.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, I really don't see it yet.
I can see Microbus A and B, real well.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, I'm going to pick out one and start marking on it.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, now I see what I'm supposed to mark on; okay.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Page 3-90 of the Flight Plan indicates that the target that Ron is aiming for is designated J-3.
Page 2-17 of the Flight Plan gives a position of J-3 as 19.948°N, 40.102°E and it is pointed out in a page from theCSM Lunar Landmark Maps.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: J-3 is a 600-metre crater outside the north rim of Carmichael.
Once landmark J-3 is out of the way, Ron will move to landmark 17-1 which is a 450-metre crater about a kilometre east of the eventual landing site.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: No, the ones I just wrote in there.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And, lets [pause] now I'm going to take a look and see if it looks like a cinder cone.
There's Maraldi Gamma, the mound sticking up there right beside Maraldi.
Hey, you guys are going to have some good hills to run down in there.
[Long pause.] Can you see it all - at all, coming up?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: That crazy radar is going to be in the way - the LM [laughter].
Okay, it's going through the landing site, now.
The shadow is just up to [pause] You can really see the scarp on there.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: See what they mean by Sculptured Hills, Gene?
See the knobby characteristics in that...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's part of the Sculpured Hills.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Now, we're just over the rim of Serenitatis, looking over the Graben Plains...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: 3, 4, 5, 6 - just got six of them on 17-1 and then the other three were were on F - F Crater; 17-1 was just barely in the - Sherlock was just barely beyond the shadow.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: This is all supposedly covered with the dark mantle, Gene, what you're seeing down there.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, the Sun angles are so that you can't tell the difference in albedo.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And look at those mare ridges, though.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I tell you, that's looking out into the gray...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think we're just about ready to climb.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think the terminator's giving you the feeling of that, maybe.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Can you see any albedo difference in the landing site area between the dark massif and the light area?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We can't see any difference between - in the low areas, between the dark mantle and other materials right now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, but Jack and I weren't really looking at the landing site.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, you could really see a difference between the - the South Massif and the - the mantle material around through there.
The mantle is not nearly as dark as it looks on the pictures, though.
But the massif, South Massif especially, looked almost a whitish color.
I guess it's because the, partly the Sun was shining on it.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Could you see anything that looks like the slide?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You can see the slide on the thing and definitely see the scarp going across through there.
I was primarily concentrating on looking for the various craters so I didn't spend that much time, you know, concentrating on how the thing looked.
But in the marks on the thing, the first about four or five marks were on F Crater and then I saw Sherlock about halfway through it and I got about five marks on the Sherlock for 17-1.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Houston, in crossing Crisium and Marginis, it looked fairly clear that with the small fresh craters, and I don't have a good size estimate right now - I think I'm looking in terms of 100 or 2 meters.
You can distinguish where blocks have been thrown up by them, or not - possibly giving depth of the local regolith from the pictures you'll get in there.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And, also, on that lineation question, not only, where the Sun grazes a - a slope do you see the horizontal lineations, but they're at the southern end of the shadowed area on a slope.
You get a couple other lineations showing up at least in a couple places I saw.
One would be parallel to the slope - that is cross-contour, and the other was at a - an angle to that direction - oh - say of about 30 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're copying you loud and clear and no problem with comm at all.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: On mag Bravo Bravo, 75 to 70 percent were utilized on J-3 and F Crater and 17-1 through the sextant.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: You started on J-3 and then you went to F Crater and...
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: I went to F Crater and then back to Sherlock or 17-1.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Actually, on J-3, I took four marks on a crater that was to the west of J-3 and then I finally saw J-3 and started marking on it.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And, there - okay; and there's a number of pictures - Now, just a minute, let me try to give you a general feeling for where they were taken.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay; a number of the last pictures that were taken on that series, or that rev, were taken between - say about 115 east, and...
[pause] oh, about 100 east and they show several examples of the light-colored or swirl...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And that's in highland country where that is a distinct gray against the tan-gray or tan highland background - general highland color.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We'd like you to move up and do the waste water dump starting now and the O2fuel cell purge.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, we'll get to that and mag Quebec Quebec is on frame 50.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, you want to dump the waste water on the front side?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: We'd like to get a start dump now, so we have it dumped prior to the camera pass, or...
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We want to get a dump prior to the UV work there, Ron.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, that's right - I'm sorry, I forgot about that.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: [Garble] how [garble] going to go, with Battery Vent to...
[pause] Okay, Battery Vented, Battery Vent is Closed and going to Dump A.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Beginning the fuel cell purge - O2
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, any objections to making that Verb 49 maneuver, now?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: America, no objection to going to Verb 49 maneuver any time.
Just do not open the UV door until the 94:45 Flight Plan time.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: 094:08:50 -This is Apollo Control at 94 hours, 8 minutes.
Apollo 17 now is maneuvering to the attitude from which the ultraviolet spectrometer and the infrared scanning radiometer experiments will be performed.
The start of those experiments on this pass will be after LOS, behind the Moon.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I think we're still in LM Pressure up there in the tunnel.
You want us to go back to LM/CM Delta-P?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'll have to wait a minute here, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: God, for a Sun - for a Sunday night, you're - you're hard to get along with.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Wise Marines end up - wise Marines end up in Antarctica.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: They don't have any gates down there.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: That sounds like a job you could probably handle.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Listen, after that answer, I'll build one down there for you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, you can go to LM/CM Delta-P.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: The fuel cell 3 purge can be terminated.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: 094:15:48 -This is Apollo Control at 94 hours, 15 minutes.
Apollo 17 is just south of the Oceans of Storm - Ocean of Storms now.
Shortly, we'll be coming up on the crater Grimaldi again.
That's where Jack Schmitt saw a flash on the last pass.
We're showing an orbit of 58.9 by 14.3 nautical miles (109.1 by 26.5 km) at the present altitude of 28.1 nautical miles (52.0 km).
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Who you talking to us with, Honeysuckle or Goldstone?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're talking through Goldst - Goldstone, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, looks like about a toss-up from here.
I'll tell you, there is really one heck of a big low-pressure area developing somewhere off the coast of California, Washington, or Canada, out in the Pacific Northwest part of the country.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We were watching it earlier today, but I tell you, now, it's really dragged in some other clouds with it.
It must cover an enormous distance and it's got some real spectacular circulation.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Just for curiosity, are you using a monocular on that?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Bob, it's - it's got a trailing front.
I can't really see the States, or even the North American continent, because...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Stand by, Jack, we'd like to - or Gene.
We'd like for you to terminate...
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, I was just going to say, it's got a - looks like a tremendous trailing front.
Roughly, north-northwest, south-southeast, and it looks like it may just sweep up the - the western coast.
It's hard to tell how far off the actual center rotation or even a front is.
I just remember from earlier this morning, when I could see landmasses, that it appeared to me to be off the Pacific Northwest out in the ocean.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We've just been kicking around with Farouk, and if you get - if you want to, during your - any of your free time, if you have any, you might look at Copernicus with your binoculars and see if the dike goes all the way - all the way across.
He would recommend using the binoculars.
Don't take any of your eat or sleep time at all, but you might get a chance on this one eat pass to, as you go by there.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: You might give me a couple minutes' warning the next time around.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'm not sure the attitude is too good for that.
I wish I'd thought of it this round.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, we were pretty busy coming up on it this time.
I think the - that's why we mentioned the attitude may be okay during the eat period.
We don't want you to break away from your eat period unless you see you can spare the time.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Just looking at the southern edge of Grimaldi, Bob, and we probably have it covered on photos, but there's a nice tangential to slightly circumferential graben along the southern wall and climbs out over the western rim and off the eastern rim, and that graben is premare - premare.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I copy on that, Jack, and as long as we're talking about Grimaldi, you might just rem - we'd like to have you brief Ron exactly on the location of that flash you saw.
We'll probably ask him to take a picture of it, maybe during one of his solo periods.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Hey, Bob, before we get awfully involved, just let me tell you what our motive is here in the next couple of hours.
That's to eat and get done what's in the Flight Plan, and come our rest period, we're probably going to turn out the lights and make sure everything's done by then so we can start it on-time tonight.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yes, that's a definite - we concur with that definitely, Gene.
Don't want you to work into your sleep period.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Gene, last night you didn't use a tone booster, and the word you gave us was that it had - it wasn't working, and we're just wondering if you want - we're thinking about working up a test board to see if - did you test it out thoroughly to make sure it wasn't working, or was that just your desire not to use it?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We tried it, Bob, and it did not work.
The only reason I'm reluctant on a test is I just don't want it to take much time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I don't think it would take much time.

The only thing I could say is it would - you know - two obvious things I'm sure you checked them, Gene, would be the utility power and make sure you had the right lamp tests on when you tried it, and that's the only - I'm sure you did it, and that's the only obvious thing - maybe a circuit breaker or something like that.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Well, we checked it both on the left side and on the right side with two separate Utility Powers and verified that the Master Alarms came on with the test on both sides, and nothing ever happened.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I was sure of that, Gene.
Just wanted to make it - put everybody at ease that it's just not working.
Just forget anything we said about tests.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Got you guys trained up there, finally.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: If I don't find my scissors here one of these days, I think it takes about four bolts on either side of the - you know the optics, where they stow the optics?
it looks like there are four little tool E bolts that'll come out.
And I think maybe they might be back behind there, I don't know.
[Pause.] See, there's a great big slot up at the top of that - oh, it's at least an inch - inch between the top of the optics thing and the top of the spacecraft.
I looked back in there with a flashlight and can't see anything, but it's a big hole back there anyhow.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: I think the commander might have something to say.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Hey, Bob, just ignore everything he said.
We'll leave him a pair of our scissors, and he's just worried about being hungry.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Those are your EVA scissors, too, aren't they?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: But, he is not - Yeah, but we can handle - we can handle it with one down there.
He is not taking the spacecraft apart to find his scissors, and I will not let him go hungry.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, Bob, before we lose you, how did the Oilers do today?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Played one of their better games, I guess.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Hey, Jack, how about if we pick you up during the eat period.
While you're eating, we'll brief you of the whole NFL situation for today.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: I guess we wait until we come around next time.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Oh - I - well, the Bears were [garble] losing but...
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Bob, we'll wait and you can get up the late evening news prepared for us here the next time around.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Have your gourmet dinner and I'll give you the news.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We're going to lose you here in about 2½ minutes.
We'll see you at 95:28, according to the Flight Plan, and you're looking good.
We don't have anything, any anomalies or anything against you right now.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We'll see you coming around the horn.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, Bob, if you could, it might be appreciated with a word or two from our home fronts.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You didn't let me do it.
I was going to give them all a call while on this LOS here, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: I bet you were concentrating on the dot.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're going to watch your signal get cut off here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Was touch and go on that because at one time they're losing that game.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: 094:41:08 -This is Apollo Control at 94 hours, 41 minutes.
We've had Loss of Signal on the third revolution.
Ron Evans performed some landmark tracking on the front side of the Moon during this pass and we got some Earth weather reports from lunar orbit, believed to be a first, in the Apollo series.
And spacecraft is in the attitude now to perform the Ultraviolet Spectrometer and the Infrared Scanning Radiometer experiments.
We - we expect to acquire Apollo 17 next at 95 hours, 28 minutes.
At 94 hours, 42 minutes; this is Mission Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 095:27:07 -This is Apollo Control at 95 hours, 27 minutes.
We should be acquiring Apollo 17 on it's fourth revolution of the Moon in about 45 seconds.
We'll stand by for the first words.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 095:28:25 -We have a signal and are receiving data.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Bob, the Flight Plan is complete through 95:30.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And Bob, the IR cover came open about 1 minute late.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: [Pause.] Might be interested, the latest tracking data has you at about 14.1 [nautical miles] perilune.
It's coming up closer to your CMC data.
[Pause.] And, America, we'd like to have the H2tank, H2tank 1, fans off.
We'll be letting that pressure decay on that.
We'll be bringing them back on prior to sleep period.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: I've got a number of PADs and Flight Plan update any time you're ready for them, if you want or you can hold off it awhile.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Why don't you start with Flight Plan updates Bob?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You should have a pen and ink change there that says UV cover, Close.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, now, just a note, or you can jump way ahead to 106:51, where it says delete, see where it says UV, Off, just delete that, or else make it a verify, because it will be off during that whole period.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Yeah, I'll just put a verified by that.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The next thing I've got are the PADs.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: First one is TEI-12, SPS G&N: 38570; plus 0.49, plus 0.92; Noun 33 times, 111:54:42.86; plus 2543.8, minus 1765.6, minus 0795.0; Roll is 186, 109, 328.
The rest of the PAD is not applicable.
Break - Break Jack, we'd like an Accept on the computer please.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: The set stars are as always, Sirius and Rigel; 133, 200, 030.
Three notes: Burn undocked, assumes no Circ, longitude of the Moon at TIGwill be minus 140.19°.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Bob, give me note two again please.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Jack, the three comments are: assumes burn undocked, assumes no Circ burn, and the Moon at TIG, the longitude will be minus 140.19°.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, I got everything, but number 2.
Rest of the PAD is NA.
Remarks: (1) burn undocked, (2) assume no Circ, (3) longitude, lunar longitude at TIGminus 140.19°, .19°.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: A full interpretation of the PAD follows:Purpose:This PAD is for an early return to Earth at the end of the twelfth lunar orbit in case an abort situation develops.Propulsion/guidance:This burn would utilise the SPS engine under the control of the G&N system.CSM weight (Noun 47):38,570 pounds (17,495 kg).Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):+0.49° and +0.92°.

These are the angles away from the longitudinal axis the SPS engine bell will be set to for the burn.Time of ignition, TIG(Noun 33):111 hours, 54 minutes, 42.86 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):X, +2,543.8 (+775.4); Y, -1,765.6 (-538.2); Z, -795.0 (-242.3).
These velocity components are expressed with respect to the local vertical.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 186°; Pitch, 109°; Yaw, 328°.

The attitude figure is with respect to the IMU orientation at the time of the burn.Overmyer notes that the remaining items on the standard form are not applicable in this case, bar one.GDC Align stars:Stars Sirius (number 15) and Rigel (number 12) would to be used for backup GDC Align in case the IMU is unavailable for this task.GDC Align angles:133°, 200° and 30°.Additional notes are that the four plus-X RCS jets would be fired for 12 seconds to settle propellants in the tanks prior to ignition, the burn would be made without a LM attached, that the CSM would still be in its present orbit and not have circularised it, and Overmyer also gives the longitude over the Moon at the moment of ignition as minus 140.19° or 140.19°W.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Good readback, Jack, and I've got the TEI-19 PAD, if you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Rest of the PAD is not applicable.
All the notes are the same as on the TEI-12 PAD, except the longitude is minus 147.62.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: And the remarks are the same as for TEI-12; that includes set stars.
And the only change is in 3, which is lunar longitude at TIGof a minus 147.62.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: A full interpretation of the PAD follows:Purpose:This PAD is for an early return to Earth at the end of the 19th lunar orbit in case an abort situation develops.Propulsion/guidance:This burn would utilise the SPS engine under the control of the G&N system.CSM weight (Noun 47):38,023 pounds (17,247 kg).Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):+0.49° and +0.86°.

These are the angles away from the longitudinal axis the SPS engine bell will be set to for the burn.Time of ignition, TIG(Noun 33):125 hours, 46 minutes, 47.22 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):X, +2,456.3 (+748.7); Y, -1,925.3 (-586.8); Z, -731.3 (-222.9).
These velocity components are expressed with respect to the local vertical.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 186°; Pitch, 108°; Yaw, 325°.

The attitude figure is with respect to the IMU orientation at the time of the burn.Overmyer tells tham that the notes appended to this PAD as the same as for TEI-12 except that the longitude over the Moon at the moment of ignition is minus 147.62° or 147.62°W.
Soon he will point out another difference, and that is that the burn does assume that he has circularised his orbit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I've got a LM DAP read-up here for you.
[Pause.] They recommend this be copied on page 1 of the LM Data Card Book, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Well, strangely enough, the LM Data Card Book's in the LM.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We'll put it in our Activation Book.
Let me find the right page.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Let me find the right page.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: LM DAP information: LM weight, 36714; CSM weight, 38078.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, the DAP load is LM weight, 36714; CSM weight, 38078.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: These numbers are required by the computer so that it can calculate the correct impulse to use when making rotation manoeuvres of the stack.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger, Jack, and I'd like to correct one call on my, the TEI PADs.
There is one other difference between the TEI-19 and the TEI-12 under notes.
And the TEI-19 assumes the circ burn, over.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, that's corrected to assume circ on the TEI-19.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I've got some notes from the Flight...
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Got some notes for Gene from the Flight Surgeon.
He promised an update to you on some meal recommendations here on food, if you'd - you might want to copy this into the supplement.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Bob, let's - let us take a look at the landing area and we'll be right back with you.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Just whenever you want it, there's no hurry on this at all.
Jack, you just might put a little note there somewhere for yourself that Copernicus is at 96.03 if you want to look at it.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: 095:45:45 -Apollo 17's coming up over the Taurus Littrow landing site now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Now we're getting some clear - look like pretty clear high-water marks on the...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: There's high-water marks all over the place there.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: ...on the north part of Tranquillitatis in here - mare onto the highlands.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, there's high-water marks all over that - that - I think that's Maraldi there.
[Pause.] Are you sure we're at 13 miles up?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: You're at 14.1 [nautical miles, 26.1 km], to be exact there, Ron.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Gee, I wonder what it feels like to be at 8 to 10.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You're going to find out, I think.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: I tell you there's some mare ridge or scarps, very sinuous - just passing one.
They not only cross the low planar areas but go right up the side of a crater in one place and a hill in another.
And it's not at all like a fault scarp.
It looks very much like a constructional ridge.
And it has the sinuousity of a, pardon the expression, rattle snake.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Does it look like that one we saw on the track the other day?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Yeah, very much like that one that you almost caught.
There's a tongute - there's a tongue depressor - several of them - grabens in here.
We must be out on the edge of Serenitatis now, right?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, we show you on the edge of Serenitatis, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: I just - we didn't get a view of the site, though, going over this time.
I think it was off to the north of us just a tad.
[Pause.] That's the best example of a mare - a mare-like ridge that was as clearly constructional as I would want to see it.
Out on the mare, it's never quite that obvious.
But there, it climbed up over a hill and then back down again.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And that was just east of the - an area just east of the edge of Serenitatis and probably a little bit south of the landing site.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Say, Bob, rather than copy specific recommended changes or deletions to a diet, can you have a general comment that's any different than what I already understand?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: If not, I would rather delay it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Two quick comments: the next couple of days, delete the peach ambrosia and the mixed fruit bar, and don't delete anything from the LM menu.
And make sure you get all the water in for rehydration, and take - bias it if you think you get 30 percent gas, then put in 30 percent more so you get all the water that is required.
And take two anti-gas tablets after each meal instead of one.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: I'll - I'll handle all those words according to their need.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're shooting magazine QQ, frame 50 - frame 50, 51, and 52.
We're taking - looking south at the terminator.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Four frames appear to be taken around this time looking towards the southwest region of Mare Serenitatis.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Ron, we need to get looking at the Pan Camera here, per the Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Power on the Pan Camera now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And has my biomed been looking all right?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: America, you can turn the Pan Camera, Off.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Will Copernicus - will be north of our track.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I believe so, from my charts here, but let me doublecheck that.
Tommy just gave me the up on that one.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: If you guys got time to listen, I can update you on the home front while you're wor - looking out the windows.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: For the CDR, they're eating beans and cornbread over at Nassau Bay tonight.
Probably a good thing they're eating beans there, Gene, because you're feeding 25 tonight.
And the horse is getting fed, also.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: It's a good thing I'm eating up here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, of course, everybody sends their love.
And over in El Lago, they're...
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: I was just going to ask you to return mine for me, would you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: They're listening to the squawk box.
You don't have to say anything to me.

And over in El Lago, Jamie's says - is saying her prayers ending them with, "God bless America and Challenger from now on." And John is saying, "There's Daddy's rocket - makes it go pitch, light out." And of course, everybody sends their love...
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: And they're all listening right now if you'd...
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Tell John, though, that I shaved the other night.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: They're all over at the - in Nassau Bay at a big meeting of the Flight Plan.
The Parkers are briefing the Cernans and the Evans on the Flight Plan tonight.
And out in Tucson, for the LMP, it's kind of cloudy and cold out there today.
Everything's working fine on the speaker out there, Jack, and they're just listening, and Mother - your Mother is really tickled and just pleased as all get out.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: And I just got a real good view of Copernicus, but I'm afraid I can't help you out on that structure in the central peak.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: But it's a fantastic sight at this altitude.
It looks like it's about 80 kilometers in diameter.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Sources quote the diameter of Copernicus as between 93 and 96 kilometres.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And put down a verify on those dark deposits in the wall.
And also on the southeast wall, there's one right on the rim.
I think we mapped that one, too, but I'll have to go back and check.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That one looked like it had a crater in it.
[Pause.] However, they all tend to be elongate radially, with respect to the crater.
But that's about all I can add now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, I'm glad, the squawk box is fixed.
And, of course, send my love and best wishes out there to Tucson.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You guys might also be interested, here in the Houston area, all of the ground voice or air-to-ground is being carried on FM stations a hundred percent of it.
And KUHT, the television station, will cover the - educational TV station - is going to cover 100 percent of the EVAs, all three of them, for all the time.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We hope we can provide them with as much education as we can entertainment.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I guess Parker's all we had left down there to brief them on the landing site, huh?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I won't even comment on that one.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: I'd like, also, to send my best up north to the Bellwood - the Bellwood area up there, because I think there's a squawk box up there, too.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, Bob, it's - all in all, it's been a pretty exciting day.
I think a pretty accomplishing day, and certainly a rewarding day on our part.
It's also been a long day, but we're hoping that it can only be superseded by tomorrow.
And judging from what we've got in store, it might very well be.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think that sums up the day.
It's a day of anticipation for what's going to happen tomorrow.
Everything's gone off real well here, and we're just glad you're in the orbit you're in and ready for tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: It's awful hard to spend much time up here anticipating.
The events come so fast and certainly are exciting and rewarding, each one, one at a time.
But obviously, tomorrow is going to be the biggy.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Ron, we're watching your 52; and just be advised you do not have to do the option 1.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Bob, I'm not sure whether it's entirely proper, being a bachelor, but I'd like to send my regards to everybody - all the families listening to squawk boxes tonight.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, we're going right over the Procellarum Ridge now, if I'm not mistaken, out in the middle of Mare Procellarum.
And we're low enough now that you can see some of the terrain.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That is - that is, some of the hills and rilles and valleys associated with that ridge system.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Break - Ron, we've got the 52, and it looks good.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Gene Cernan's remark about up north in Bellwood is a reference to his mother, Mrs.
Andrew Cernan, who is following the course of the mission at her home in Bellwood, Illinois.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The GDC is aligned, and we verified that the LM valve is in LM/CM Delta-P.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Ron, we're ready to uplink the jet monitor program and one of the burn constants.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: While you're eating or getting ready to eat, I can update some of the news with you tonight, if you're away from the windows.
Or are you all still looking out?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: There's not a whole lot of news on the wire tonight, matter of fact.
President Nixon received a firsthand report on private Vietnam peace negotiations today from a key member of the US negotiating team, General Alexander Haig, Jr., Henry Kissinger's Chief Lieutenant.
Haig flew back from Paris to report to the President, while technical experts from both sides held an unusual Sunday meeting in the French capital.
Presidential Assistant Kissinger will resume his talks with North Vietnam's Le Duc Tho on Monday after a 1-day recess.
And former President Harry Truman continued to show some signs of improvement late today after he was earlier removed from the critical list at Research Hospital up - up in Kansas City.
His cardiac situation has improved, and this improvement included a slower and stronger pulse and a stable blood pressure.

The football scores are as follows in the National Football League today: The Bears defeated the Eagles 21 to 12; the Detroit Lions and the Buffalo Bills played to a 21-21 stand-off; Dolphins extended their undefeated streak 23 to 13 over the New York Giants; the Patriots defeated the New Orleans Saints 17 to 10; Green Bay sewed up the Central Division of the NFC by defeating the Vikings 23 to 7; the Cardinals upset the Rams today 24 to 14; Denver defeated San Diego 38-13; Kansas City upset Baltimore 24 to 10; the 49ers defeated the Falcons 20 to nothing, 20 to 0; and Pittsburg defeated Houston 9 to 3, and they - they took the Central Division of the AFC.
So the playoff picture is becoming a little clearer.
You've got - in the NFC, you've got Washington, Dallas, and Green Bay.
And the fourth team will either be San Francisco, Los Angeles, or Atlanta.
That will be decided next week.
In the AFC, Pittsburg, Miami, and Oakland are in it.
And the fourth team will be either the Jets [pause] and another team.
They've got the Giants listed here, but it can't be the Giants because that's the wrong division.
So we'll - we'll check that one out.
The fourth team is either going to be Cleveland or the Jets.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: And did you get a spurious Master Caution and Warning?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Ron was retesting our bleeper again.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And it's one of those that works in lunar orbit, I guess.
It doesn't work during coast because it's working now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Sounds like there's going to be some good football games coming up.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's the Oakland - Oakland and the Jets.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: The EMP is running, and the computer's yours.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Ron, we'd like the H2Tank Fans, On, and that will be the sleep configuration.
H2Tank 1 Fans, On, [pause] and leave 3 in Auto.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: You want H2Tank 1 - You want 1 and 2, On?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, that leaves us with Tank 1, On; Tank 2, Off; and Tank 3 in Auto.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: If you're calling the H2fans; that's what we want.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Yeah, H2Fans, Bob; I'll say it again.
H2Fans: 1 is On, 2 is Off, and 3 is Auto.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's a good configuration, and that will be the sleep configuration.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Hey, Bob, it looks like we might make - getting to sleep on time tonight, and - well, we will make it.
And I think it goes without saying, we definitely want to get up on time tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm going to have the tone booster plugged in, and also I want to make sure the suit power and audio power and all that stuff is on.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: If Ron's listening, just some words about the Mapping Camera extend/retract times.
We came up with slightly different times than what he'd called and we were wondering if his were just ballpark or whether he had timed it?

In either case, the times are a little bit long on extend/retract which is a little cause for worry about that - the Mapping Camera may fail and later on downstream, we may want to change our operating mode on that Mapping Camera and he might want to time it a little closer.
If he - if he can - the chance should come up.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Hey, Bob, that's a good point on the thing.
Those weren't exact times and, as matter of fact, we looked - kind of looked away and it was about in - you know, somewhere around that - that period of time.
We looked away and when - when we looked back, it was gray again, so - next...
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: ...next time we extend them, I'll get a good accurate indication.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: We'll take the strip chart data.
We've got 01:24; 1 minute, 24 seconds for extend and 01:51; 1 minute, 51 seconds for retract.
And both those are a little bit on the high side.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: I would believe the strip charts.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: You all are about 4 minutes from LOS.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, we might lose you a little bit earlier than that.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, we may lose you a little early due to the SIM bay attitude and we'll pick you up at 97:22.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: 096:34:22 -This is Apollo Control at 96 hours, 34 minutes.
Apollo 17 has turned the corner a little early, about 50 seconds early.
We've lost contact with the antenna because of it's attitude, about 50 seconds early.
During this fourth pass on the front side of the Moon there was considerable description of the lunar surface and photography was accomplished.
The Mapping Camera experts will continue to study the extension and retraction times of the camera.
If those times continue to grow, they will probably revise the Flight Plan somewhat to reduce the number of times that camera must be extended and retracted.
At the present time though they're just keeping a close eye on those times to see whether they will continue to grow.
We'll next acquire Apollo 17 on it's fifth revolution of the Moon at 97 hours, 22 minutes.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 097:21:25 -This is Apollo control at 97 hours, 21 minutes.
We're 50 seconds away from the time Apollo 17 should be within communications range on it's fifth revolution of the Moon.
We'll stand by for first contact.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're just about ready to finish up here.
I have a couple of questions.
Do you want us to cycle the cryo fans or just leave them alone now?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We'd like them just left alone, left in your configuration you are now, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, there will be no cycling then.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Who's got the duty tonight or will he be wearing a headset?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Yeah, he - Ron has the duty.
He will be wearing a headset, but I will be on the biomed underneath.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It's an addition to that Flight Surgeon's note.
When you rehydrate your food, make sure that you go the - let it rehydrate for the maximum amount of time.
I know you miss it on this one, but the next - like tomorrow morning, make sure it rehydrates the maximum amount of time.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: And we got the star crater stereo trio.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And that - we're - mag - Oscar Oscar is on 93 now.
The last three pictures were that trio.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The 'star crater' that Jack refers to is a bright, relatively fresh 1.5-km ray crater on the northeast rim of the farside crater Chaplygin.
It is now called Chaplygin B.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 097:28:20 -This is Apollo control at 97 hours, 28 minutes.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Bob, we're essentially complete on the presleep checklist.
And except for computing communications configuration and Ron will take care of that.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And, Bob, while we're gradually getting in configuration here, let me reiterate something that I've been watching this rev as we did a lot of other things.

And that was this relationship of the light-colored or light gray swirl patterns on the surface to patterns - associated pattern - parallel patterns that are darker than the average of the surrounding area.
And this is true both in Mare Marginis and in most cases on the back side.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We'll get that to the PI.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Roughly, although they're very irregular patterns - roughly it's concentric zoning of dark to light within a intermediate albedo surface.
Now there are variations on that theme; sometimes you don't get the symmetry quite as good, but it's common enough that I think it's worth noting.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Also, there's a sequence of different kinds of crater filling on the far side, and I think that, as the orbital stay progresses, we may be able to pin down the relative age relationships and the characteristics of those crater fill - filling episodes.

Whether they are single episodes that happen in a variety of crater or they're a function of the age and characteristics of the craters in which you find them is not clear right now, but the - they seem to form fairly distinct groupings of crater-fill material.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: One of those crater-fill materials that you also see in other kinds of depressions other than craters is a very smooth, light, plains-forming material.

And it is, although cratered, when you see it at the terminator, it is smoother than the mare; that is, it does not seem to have the swell, the sea swell characteristics or ridges or any other features other than the crater's superimposed on it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: 097:33:12 -Apollo 17's orbit now 59.2 by 13.7 nautical miles [109.6 by 25.4 km].
The present altitude, 17.8 nautical miles [33.0 km].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: You can go ahead and close the UV cover.
We'd like to hold off 1 minute before you turn the UV off.
We want to look at 1 minute of data with the cover closed.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, We'll give you a cue when you go UV, Off, and INCO would like to know what you've got on your High Gain pitch and yaw knobs.
Not the dials but the knobs.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: The knobs are - about plus 20 and 185.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: A Pitch of plus 20 and 185 on the Yaw.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: And, Jack, you can turn the UV off now.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, I got the landing site.
We're right over the top of it, and the scarp is fantastically detailed at this - Can you see in there, Gene?
Right down, right down, straight down there.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, well, the sli - the light mantle is ob - very obviously mantling the area.
The scarp was very detailed, and, so far, could not see any structure in the massifs at all, but I haven't had any - didn't have much time to watch it on that pass.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The slide very definitely subdued the general detail in the plains area - or the light mantle, if you will, rather than slide.
[Pause.] MOCR Crater was finally out of the dark.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, we'd like to know if you think you can adjust the High Gain as close to plus 15 and Yaw 190 as possible?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, Bob, that's adjusted plus 15 and 190, and I suspect I was a little closer to 15 than 20 when I called you before.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'll tell you, from this altitude and with that low Sun, there's no question of the sharpness of the topographic features in the landing area.

The - the scarp, and even some of the apparent back - pardon - backflow features - and Parker will know what I'm talking about - that is apparent flows to the west in the light mantle area were extremely sharp, even those fronts going west were sharp.
It looked even more like a mare ridge than it ever did before.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I had a - just a quick view of the site, and if we're anywhere near it, we'll recognize it, I think, without question.
And, I think with that, we'll bid farewell and good night.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And looking for a busy day tomorrow - -
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Go ahead; I cut you out.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: I say never fear, your CMP is watching.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, okay, as soon as I get bedded down, I'll do that.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Don't worry, he's got a batch of guys up here reminding him of that one.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And I've checked out the little whistle, and it works like a charm.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Just to make sure, I'm going to check it again.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: ...you guys to get that switch last night, and I lost my bet.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: And, Robert, good night to all.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: This is a test, test, test.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We said goodnight at 97 hours, 47 minutes.
We'll leave the line up for a few minutes longer, to see whether there is any further conversation.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 097:53:55 -This is Apollo Control at 97 hours, 53 minutes.

Crew has just turned off the voice switch on the spacecraft, indicating they do not intend to talk anymore tonight, and we do not intend to put in anymore calls to them, so we'll take this line down now and come back up with hourly reports, during the sleep period, which has just started.
transcript.speaker: PAO
